did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study compared the diagnostic performance of human readers to that of a trained convolutional neural network (CNN) for the diagnosis of lentigo maligna using dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The study presents original experimental results from a dermoscopy masterclass where participants classified lesions at baseline and after training, comparing their diagnostic performance to a trained convolutional neural network. It reports specific metrics such as sensitivity and proportions of correct answers, indicating it is an original diagnostic accuracy study rather than a review.

title: The dermoscopic inverse approach significantly improves the accuracy of human readers for lentigo maligna diagnosis
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology
brief_summary: This study evaluated whether training on the dermoscopic inverse approach improves the diagnostic accuracy of human readers for early lentigo maligna compared to classic pattern analysis. The researchers utilized clinical and dermoscopic images of histopathologically diagnosed lentigo maligna, pigmented actinic keratoses, and solar lentigo/flat seborrheic keratoses. Participants in a dermoscopy masterclass classified lesions at baseline and after training, with their performance compared against a trained convolutional neural network. The results showed that mean sensitivity for lentigo maligna increased from 51.5% without training to 56.7% after pattern analysis training, and significantly to 83.6% after learning the inverse approach. Furthermore, the proportion of readers outperforming the AI model rose from 6.4% to 53.9% following the inverse approach training. The study concludes that adding the inverse approach to classic pattern analysis significantly enhances human diagnostic sensitivity for early lentigo maligna.

lesion_types_studied.details: Histopathologically diagnosed early lentigo maligna images were used as the primary target for diagnosis in the dermoscopy masterclass and compared against a CNN.
Histopathologically diagnosed pigmented actinic keratosis images were included in the dataset to distinguish from lentigo maligna.
Histopathologically diagnosed solar lentigo and flat seborrheic keratosis images were included in the dataset to distinguish from lentigo maligna.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories (Lentigo maligna vs. pigmented actinic keratosis vs. solar lentigo/flat seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The study involves a trained convolutional neural network (CNN) and human readers classifying dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories (LM, PAK, SL/SK). The CNN predicts lesion classes, and its performance is evaluated via sensitivity and proportion of correct answers, which are classification metrics.

datasets_used.name: Dermoscopy Masterclass Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of clinical and dermoscopic images of histopathologically diagnosed lesions used in a dermoscopy masterclass study. The dataset included images of lentigo maligna, pigmented actinic keratosis, and solar lentigo/flat seborrheic keratosis.

use.training: Used for training human readers on pattern analysis and the inverse approach.
use.testing: Used for testing human readers' diagnostic accuracy at baseline and after training, and for comparison against a trained convolutional neural network.
use.other: Used to train a convolutional neural network (CNN) for comparison purposes.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: percentages of readers outperforming the convolutional neural network

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: Training on the inverse approach does not increase the diagnostic accuracy (specifically sensitivity) of human readers for early lentigo maligna compared to baseline or classic pattern analysis.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: Training on the inverse approach significantly increases the diagnostic accuracy (specifically sensitivity) of human readers for early lentigo maligna compared to baseline or classic pattern analysis.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study concludes that the inverse approach 'significantly improves' the sensitivity of human readers, based on the increase in mean sensitivity from 51.5% (baseline) to 83.6% (after inverse approach training). However, specific p-values, confidence intervals, or the name of the statistical test used to determine this significance are not reported in the provided text.

metric: Percentage of readers outperforming the convolutional neural network (CNN)

Trained Convolutional Neural Network (CN.Note: Used as a baseline for comparison; specific metric values for the CNN alone are not reported, only the percentage of human readers outperforming it.

Dermoscopy Masterclass Dataset.notes: The inverse approach, added to classic pattern analysis, significantly improved the sensitivity of human readers for early LM diagnosis. The proportion of readers outperforming the AI model rose significantly from 6.4% at baseline to 53.9% after learning the inverse approach.

conclusions: The dermoscopic inverse approach, when added to classic pattern analysis, significantly improves the sensitivity of human readers for the diagnosis of early lentigo maligna.

Training on the inverse approach increased the mean sensitivity for lentigo maligna from 51.5% (baseline) to 56.7% (after pattern analysis training) and significantly to 83.6% (after inverse approach training).
The proportion of human readers outperforming the trained convolutional neural network increased from 6.4% at baseline to 53.9% after learning the inverse approach.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the experimental setting and the inclusion of only histopathologically diagnosed lesions, which may limit generalizability to real-world clinical scenarios with ambiguous cases.

The diagnostic performance of the trained convolutional neural network (CNN) is used as a benchmark, but specific performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity, specificity) for the CNN itself are not reported in the provided text, only the percentage of human readers outperforming it.
The study design relies on a dermoscopy masterclass setting, which may introduce selection bias as participants are likely experienced dermatologists rather than a general population of clinicians.
No conflicts of interest were disclosed, and funding sources were none, suggesting no apparent financial bias in the study outcomes.
